data.title: Pinterest (PINS) Stock Looks Discounted After A 72% Fall Yet Rich On Earnings
data.description:
Pinterest has seen its share price fall 71.8% over the past five years, and the latest valuation checks suggest the stock still screens as expensive rather than a clear bargain despite that pullback.

  • Over the past 5 years, Pinterest shareholders have seen the stock decline 71.8%, which puts more focus on whether today’s price now reflects the company’s risks and opportunities.
  • On the upside, TD Cowen points to the potential for Pinterest’s Performance+ advertising solutions to support future revenue growth, while recent insider selling of about US$8 million in stock may signal a degree of caution from within the company.
  • With a value score of 2 out of 6, Pinterest does not come across as a clear bargain on the broader valuation checks.

The issue now is whether Pinterest’s current share price offers enough compensation for its recent performance and mixed valuation signals.

Find out why Pinterest's -38.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Has Pinterest Run Too Far on Earnings?

The P/E ratio is a useful way to look at Pinterest because the company is reporting positive earnings that can be compared directly with its share price. Pinterest currently trades on a P/E of about 36.7x, which is well above the Interactive Media and Services industry average of 14.3x and also above the peer group average of 29.3x.

On Simply Wall St’s fair multiple framework, which looks at Pinterest’s growth profile, margins, size and risk, a P/E of roughly 26.8x would be more in line with those fundamentals. That puts the current valuation at a clear premium to where this model suggests the earnings multiple might sit. TD Cowen has also highlighted growth potential tied to Performance+ advertising solutions by 2026. However, the present P/E still implies investors are paying a premium for that potential when compared with both peers and this tailored benchmark.

On the P/E measure, Pinterest stock currently appears overvalued relative to both its industry and a more tailored fair multiple estimate.

See what the numbers say about this price — find out in our valuation breakdown.

The Pinterest Narrative: What Would Justify Today's Price?

Simply Wall St Narratives for Pinterest pick up where the valuation puzzle leaves off by laying out the specific combinations of future growth, margins and earnings that would need to hold for Pinterest’s stock to be worth materially more, or less, than today’s price on Simply Wall St's Community page. Where a single ratio or model offers one number, these narratives describe the future that number relies on so you can follow whether reality stays close to that path over time.

The Pinterest community is split between those who see AI and ad tech creating upside and those who focus on ad pricing and execution risks.

Bull case: 21% undervalued

"Ongoing advancements in AI-driven visual search and personalized content curation, supported by proprietary multimodal and generative retrieval models, are associated with deeper user engagement and increased session times. This may drive higher average revenue per user (ARPU) and lift both revenue and margins over time…"

Read the full Bull Case to see why Pinterest could be undervalued

Bear case: roughly fairly valued

"The decline in ad pricing by 18% year-on-year might pressure net revenues and lower profit margins despite increased ad impressions…"

Read the full Bear Case to see why Pinterest could be overvalued

Do you think there's more to the story for Pinterest? Head over to our Community to see what others are saying!

The Bottom Line

For Pinterest, the current picture suggests it may be overvalued on market multiples, with the P/E sitting well above both industry averages and a tailored fair multiple estimate. That leaves less room for error if expectations around monetisation and ad tech benefits do not play out as hoped. After a difficult 5 year return profile, the key question is whether Pinterest can grow earnings and margins into this premium, or whether the market eventually resets the multiple closer to peers if execution or ad pricing disappoints.

data.title: Insider Spends US$503k Buying More Shares In CarMax
data.description:
Those following along with CarMax, Inc. (NYSE:KMX) will no doubt be intrigued by the recent purchase of shares by Mark O'Neil, Lead Independent Director of the company, who spent a stonking US$503k on stock at an average price of US$52.36. Aside from being a solid chunk in its own right, the deft move also saw their holding increase by some 35%.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

The Last 12 Months Of Insider Transactions At CarMax

In fact, the recent purchase by Mark O'Neil was the biggest purchase of CarMax shares made by an insider individual in the last twelve months, according to our records. That means that an insider was happy to buy shares at above the current price of US$51.82. While their view may have changed since the purchase was made, this does at least suggest they have had confidence in the company's future. To us, it's very important to consider the price insiders pay for shares. As a general rule, we feel more positive about a stock if insiders have bought shares at above current prices, because that suggests they viewed the stock as good value, even at a higher price.

In the last twelve months CarMax insiders were buying shares, but not selling. You can see the insider transactions (by companies and individuals) over the last year depicted in the chart below. If you click on the chart, you can see all the individual transactions, including the share price, individual, and the date!

Check out our latest analysis for CarMax

CarMax is not the only stock that insiders are buying. For those who like to find small cap companies at attractive valuations, this free list of growing companies with recent insider purchasing, could be just the ticket.

Does CarMax Boast High Insider Ownership?

For a common shareholder, it is worth checking how many shares are held by company insiders. We usually like to see fairly high levels of insider ownership. Insiders own 0.5% of CarMax shares, worth about US$37m. We've certainly seen higher levels of insider ownership elsewhere, but these holdings are enough to suggest alignment between insiders and the other shareholders.

What Might The Insider Transactions At CarMax Tell Us?

It's certainly positive to see the recent insider purchases. And the longer term insider transactions also give us confidence. When combined with notable insider ownership, these factors suggest CarMax insiders are well aligned, and that they may think the share price is too low. While it's good to be aware of what's going on with the insider's ownership and transactions, we make sure to also consider what risks are facing a stock before making any investment decision. For instance, we've identified 3 warning signs for CarMax (1 doesn't sit too well with us) you should be aware of.

But note: CarMax may not be the best stock to buy. So take a peek at this free list of interesting companies with high ROE and low debt.

data.title: Seven Elements Investment Holdings (SEHK:1660) Stock Small Profit Challenges Longstanding Loss Narratives
data.description:
Seven Elements Investment Holdings (SEHK:1660) has reported its FY 2026 first half results with revenue of HK$84.6 million and basic EPS of HK$0.000063, setting a cautious tone for investors tracking its profitability. The company has seen revenue move from HK$110.8 million in the first half of FY 2025 to HK$87.1 million in the second half of FY 2025 and HK$84.6 million in the latest half. Net income shifted from a loss of HK$7.4 million and HK$70.4 million in those prior halves to a profit of HK$0.4 million this period, highlighting tight margins at a share price of HK$0.228. Overall, the results point to fragile profitability where small changes in costs or revenue can quickly affect margins.

See our full analysis for Seven Elements Investment Holdings.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing market narratives around growth potential, risk, and the durability of Seven Elements Investment Holdings's margins.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

HK$171.7m trailing revenue alongside continuing losses

  • Over the last twelve months, Seven Elements Investment Holdings booked HK$171.7 million in revenue with a net loss of HK$10.1 million, showing that a full year of sales has not yet translated into positive profitability.
  • Bears argue the business is struggling to convert its diversified operations into profits, and the trailing figures align with that concern as losses over the past five years have reportedly worsened at about 60.8% per year, while recent halves such as FY 2025 H2 also showed a loss of HK$70.4 million.
    • This pattern of larger historical losses, alongside a trailing loss of HK$10.1 million on HK$171.7 million of revenue, heavily supports the bearish view that earnings quality has been under pressure for an extended period.
    • At the same time, the move from a FY 2025 H2 loss of HK$70.4 million to a FY 2026 H1 profit of HK$0.4 million introduces a point of tension for bears, because the most recent half year result is modestly profitable even though the longer lookback remains loss making.

Small FY 2026 H1 profit against weaker five year track record

  • In FY 2026 H1, Seven Elements Investment Holdings reported net income of HK$0.4 million and basic EPS of HK$0.000063, compared with losses of HK$7.4 million in FY 2025 H1 and HK$70.4 million in FY 2025 H2, which underlines how thin the latest profit is compared with the size of prior half year losses.

Canadian denosumab launch puts Shanghai Henlius Biotech in focus

Shanghai Henlius Biotech (SEHK:2696) is drawing attention after announcing that its denosumab biosimilars BILDYOS and TUZEMTY are now available in Canada through partner Organon, covering all indications of the reference products.

The launch extends Henlius’s reach beyond its primarily China based revenue by bringing its osteoporosis and oncology supportive care portfolio into a mature biologics market, where Organon holds exclusive commercialization rights outside China.

See our latest analysis for Shanghai Henlius Biotech.

The Canada launch has arrived during a strong run in Shanghai Henlius Biotech’s stock, with a 1 day share price return of 6.68% and 7 day share price return of 15.75%, while the 90 day share price return is down 10.31%. With Shanghai Henlius Biotech posting a recent share price rebound, trading at HK$68.70 and sitting well below analyst targets and some intrinsic estimates, the key question now is whether there is real value left or if the market is already pricing in future growth.

Preferred P/E of 39.1x for Shanghai Henlius Biotech: Is it justified?

Shanghai Henlius Biotech is trading on a P/E of 39.1x, which, when set against the last close at HK$68.70, points to a stock that the market is pricing at a premium to its earnings compared with peers.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price with earnings per share, and for a biotech company like Shanghai Henlius Biotech it can reflect how much investors are willing to pay today for each unit of current profitability. A higher P/E can suggest strong confidence in future earnings, but it can also mean expectations are already demanding.

Here, the picture is mixed. On one hand, Shanghai Henlius Biotech is described as having high quality earnings and has moved from losses to profitability over the past 5 years, with earnings growing strongly over that longer period. On the other hand, earnings growth over the past year was 0.8%, noticeably below both its own 5 year average rate and below the 8.1% growth reported for the wider Biotechs industry, which means the current P/E embeds faster improvement than the most recent year shows.

Compared with the Asian Biotechs industry average P/E of 30.6x, Shanghai Henlius Biotech is trading on a meaningfully richer multiple of 39.1x. It is also described as expensive versus an estimated fair P/E of 17x, suggesting the valuation is well above the level that historical relationships would point to as a potential anchor if sentiment cools.

data.title: Founder Led Stocks Retail Investors Are Watching In Australia Right Now
data.description:
With inflation trends shifting, central banks reassessing rate paths and growth signals looking mixed, many investors are looking for leaders who are personally tied to the long term success of their companies. Founder led stocks can offer that kind of alignment because the people calling the shots often have much of their own wealth on the line too. This article uses a Founder Led Companies screener that focuses on that commitment. It then highlights 3 stocks from the list that stand out and are worth a closer look if you are interested in investing in legacies, not just executives.

Advertisement

Flight Centre Travel Group (ASX:FLT)

Overview: Flight Centre Travel Group is a global travel retailer that connects leisure and corporate customers with flights, accommodation, tours and cruises, complemented by services like foreign exchange and employee benefits, across brands centered on the Flight Centre name. Its network spans Australia, New Zealand, the Americas, Europe, the Middle East, Africa and Asia, combining in-store, online and corporate travel solutions.

Operations: Flight Centre Travel Group generates most of its A$2.9b revenue from Leisure at about A$1.4b and Corporate at about A$1.2b, with Australia and New Zealand contributing roughly A$1.5b of sales.

Market Cap: A$2.4b

Flight Centre Travel Group attracts interest because it sits at the intersection of global travel demand and a founder led culture that is still pushing hard on digital platforms and AI to improve efficiency and margins. Analysts expect faster earnings growth than the broader Australian market and see potential upside from current P/E levels. Ongoing buybacks of up to A$200 million indicate management is willing to commit capital alongside shareholders. At the same time, the company contends with thin profit margins, a high reliance on external borrowing and pressure on some regions and leisure earnings. How those factors evolve is a key source of both opportunity and risk for long term investors who want leaders with significant personal ownership.

Flight Centre Travel Group is pushing hard on digital and AI, yet its thin margins, borrowings and buybacks leave a lot beneath the surface. See how the story fits together in the analysis report for Flight Centre Travel Group.

Macquarie Technology Group (ASX:MAQ)

Overview: Macquarie Technology Group is an Australian provider of telecoms, cloud computing, cybersecurity and data centre services, helping corporate and government customers run secure networks, store and manage data, and keep critical systems online. Its offerings range from voice and video communications to managed cloud, disaster recovery, data backup and cyber protection, all delivered from its Sydney base.

Operations: Macquarie Technology Group generates most of its A$379.4m revenue from Cloud Services & Government at about A$223.9m, followed by Telecom at roughly A$108.2m and Data Centres at around A$83.6m, with inter segment eliminations of A$36.3m, all from Australia.

Market Cap: A$1.8b

Macquarie Technology Group stands out for investors watching founder led stocks that sit at the heart of Australia’s digital infrastructure, from secure government cloud to data centres and connectivity. Some analysts have highlighted potential for the share price to rise from current levels; however, available forecasts also indicate relatively modest earnings and revenue growth, a 6.6% return on equity and funding that leans entirely on external borrowing. These factors can prompt questions about how efficiently that premium P/E is being used to build long term value. For investors, the appeal lies in a focused, long tenured management team and exposure to essential digital services, set against the need to weigh the pace of expected growth and funding risk against that potential.

Macquarie Technology Group sits at the crossroads of premium P/E expectations and fully debt funded growth, and the real story is how that trade off could play out for shareholders. Get the full picture in the 1 key reward and 1 important major warning sign

Mesoblast (ASX:MSB)

Overview: Mesoblast develops regenerative medicine therapies based on mesenchymal lineage cells, aiming to treat severe inflammatory and cardiovascular conditions such as graft versus host disease, inflammatory bowel disease, chronic heart failure, chronic low back pain and rheumatoid arthritis, supported by a series of partnerships with global pharmaceutical groups and research organisations.

Operations: Mesoblast currently generates its US$65.4m in revenue from the development and commercialization of its cell technology platform.

Market Cap: A$2.6b

Mesoblast attracts attention because it is trying to turn a large pipeline of cell therapies into a commercial business, with Ryoncil already approved in the U.S. and multiple late stage programs in areas like chronic low back pain and heart failure that target sizeable patient groups and align with demand for non opioid options. Analysts currently forecast increases in revenue and earnings and a higher future return on equity, yet the company is still loss making, trades on a relatively high P/S multiple and relies on external funding, including a recent US$50m facility from an existing director. For investors who can tolerate execution and regulatory risk, the combination of first mover cell therapy assets and ambitious forecasts makes Mesoblast a story that some market participants continue to monitor closely.

Mesoblast’s push to turn a broad cell therapy pipeline into real revenue is accelerating, yet the real tension is between those ambitious analyst forecasts and the risks of a still loss making business.
